config.title: Berlin Transport Strike — FAQ
config.subtitle: Quick answers for tourists keeping a Berlin day moving during strikes and disruptions.

items.q: What should tourists check first during a Berlin transport strike?
items.a: Check which transport layer is affected before changing the whole day. BVG covers U-Bahn, tram, bus and ferry. S-Bahn Berlin covers the S-Bahn. For mixed routes, airport journeys and regional trains, use VBB, BER or DB.

items.q: Are BVG and S-Bahn the same during a Berlin strike?
items.a: No. BVG operates U-Bahn, tram, bus and ferry services, while S-Bahn and regional trains are separate. A BVG strike may still leave S-Bahn or regional trains useful, and an S-Bahn disruption may still leave U-Bahn, tram and bus routes working.

items.q: Can I just walk if transport is disrupted in central Berlin?
items.a: Often, yes. If you are around Alexanderplatz, Museum Island, Hackescher Markt, Unter den Linden or Hauptbahnhof, walking can be a real sightseeing plan, not just a backup. It is not the answer for every hotel or airport journey, but in the centre it can save the day.

items.q: How early should I leave for BER Airport during a disruption?
items.a: Leave earlier than a normal day and check BER, VBB, S-Bahn or DB before leaving. If Airport Express, regional rail or S-Bahn options are unstable, compare express buses and taxi or ride-hail options before the last safe train window disappears.

items.q: Is a taxi always the best backup in Berlin?
items.a: No. A taxi can be the right choice for a flight, luggage, kids, mobility needs or a tight deadline, but during citywide disruption roads and pickup points can also get worse. Often the best plan is rail to a nearby hub, then walking, bike, scooter, bus or taxi for the final stretch.

items.q: Can I get compensation for delays in Berlin?
items.a: Passenger-rights claims depend on the operator, ticket type and length of delay. DB publishes passenger-rights guidance for DB-operated services including S-Bahn, RB, RE, IC and ICE. Keep your ticket, screenshots and disruption proof if a serious delay affects a paid onward journey.

items.q: Can I still reach the BerlinWalk tour during transport disruption?
items.a: Usually yes, but leave extra time. The tour starts at Alexanderplatz and lasts about **2 hours**. That area has U-Bahn, S-Bahn, tram, bus and walkable central-street options, so a problem on one layer often still leaves another way in.
